coredata.dc:title: Green one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of lemon yellow in soft drinks
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Ma, Yuan
Zhou, You
Xiao, Rong-Dan

Wu, Lin-Hong
Zhao, De-Hong
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescent sensor for the determination of lemon yellow was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), which were prepared via a hydrothermal method with dried pomelo peel and L-tyrosine. The N-CQDs exhibited the blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 28 %. The sensing principle of N-CQDs was quenched by lemon yellow via static quenching. The potential interfering substances showed no influence on the detection of lemon yellow. The limit of detection was 0.023 mg/L and lower than that of national standard. Furthermore, the synthesized N-CQDs have been successfully applied to the measurement of lemon yellow in real samples. Hence, the N-CQDs would be a promising sensor in food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Lemon yellow
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of lemon yellow in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N-CQDs; (b) size distribution of N-CQDs; (c) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs (pomelo peel) and N-CQDs (pomelo peel and L-tyrosine).
(a) XPS spectra of the N-CQDs; XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) spectra.
(a) UV–vis absorbance (green), excitation (blue), emission (red) spectra of N-CQDs; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Selectivity analysis for lemon yellow detection; (b) other potential interferences.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs in various concentrations of lemon yellow. (b) Relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of N-CQDs and the concentration of lemon yellow (0–0.369 mg/mL); inset: the linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of lemon yellow (0–0.369 mg/mL). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs and N-CQDs-lemon yellow system. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of N-CQDs.
Determination of lemon yellow in real drink sample (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Green one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of lemon yellow in soft drinks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jia Sixie College of Agriculture, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Shouguang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Weifang University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jia Sixie College of Agriculture, Weifang University of Science and Technology, Shouguang, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent sensor for the determination of lemon yellow was developed based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), which were prepared via a hydrothermal method with dried pomelo peel and L-tyrosine. The N-CQDs exhibited the blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 28 %. The sensing principle of N-CQDs was quenched by lemon yellow via static quenching. The potential interfering substances showed no influence on the detection of lemon yellow. The limit of detection was 0.023 mg/L and lower than that of national standard. Furthermore, the synthesized N-CQDs have been successfully applied to the measurement of lemon yellow in real samples. Hence, the N-CQDs would be a promising sensor in food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As an edible synthetic pigment, lemon yellow is one of the common food additives [1,2]. However, excessive consumption of lemon yellow will seriously endanger human health. Studies have shown that excessive intake of lemon yellow will cause DNA damage and interfere with DNA synthesis, resulting in fetal malformation [3]. It can even be converted into carcinogens, causing subcutaneous sarcoma, liver cancer, colon cancer, etc. [4] Therefore, it is necessary to detect the content of lemon yellow in food.

At present, many analytical methods have been reported for precise analysis of food additives, such as high performance liquid chromatography [5], electrochemistry [6], photoelectrochemistry [7] and spectrophotometry [8]. Spectrophotometry requires less equipment and lower detection cost, but they often require more complicated sample pretreatment to obtain better results [9,10]. The high performance liquid chromatography has the advantages of sensitive detection, high accuracy and good quantitative reproducibility, but the pretreatment is complicated and unsuitable for further use [9,10]. Though these methods provide accurate results, the high cost and complex operation process limit their development in practical application. In contrast, fluorescence methods have aroused special interest due to their simple operation, low-cost, great selectivity, sensitivity and fast response, etc. [11–14].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted extensive attention as a new generation of materials, which have some fascinating properties such as low cost, low toxicity, facile preparation, strong fluorescence performances, good chemical stability, tunable photoluminescence, ease of functionalization and high biocompatibility [15–17]. CQDs have become the excellent candidate among numerous fluorescent materials and have been widely used in many fields including bioimaging [18], chemical and biological sensing [19,20], biomedical materials [21], fluorescence tracing [22], and photocatalysis fields [23], etc.

D-isoascorbic acid, sodium chloride, potassium sulfate, glycine, disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate, sucrose, lemon yellow, vanillin, magnesium chloride, sodium acetate, barium chloride, potassium nitrate, rose essence powder, cyclamate, aluminum trichloride hexahydrate, composite amino acids, urea, potassium chloride, lemon yellow, sucralose trichloride, anhydrous glucose, L-cysteine, anhydrous calcium chloride reduced glutathione, sunset yellow, copper acetate, ferrous sulfate, zinc chloride were obtained from Komio reagent (Tianjin, China). All reagents are analytical grade and used as received. For all the experiments, ultra-pure water is used. 1 mg/mL stock solution was prepared in ultra-pure water and kept at 4 °C before use.

The TEM image was acquired using a Talos F200S G2 (USA). The sample for TEM was prepared by adding a drop of CQDs solution to the Agar carbon-coated 400 mesh Cu grid. The FT-IR spectra were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a FrontierFT-IRSpectrometer (USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a Thermos Fisher ESCALAB Xi+ (UK). The Fluorescence was measured by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (USA). The UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs sample were recorded on a Cintra 4040 (Australia).

N-CQDs were prepared via a hydrothermal method with dried pomelo peel and L-tyrosine as carbon source. Briefly, 0.30 g of pomelo peel was stirred with 0.30 g L-tyrosine in 10 mL ultra-pure H2O. The solution was placed into a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor (25 mL) and heated at 200 °C for a period of 6 h in an oven. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered and purified through a membrane filter (0.22 μm) for further use.

Quinine sulfate was selected as the reference fluorophore to measure quantum yield. The quinine sulfate solution (quantum yield 54 %) was used as reference in 0.1 mol·L-1 H2SO4 solution. Quantum yield of N-CQDs was determined at an optimized excitation wavelength of 330 nm using the following formula [24,25]:
Ψ = ΨR × (I/A)/(IR/AR) × (η/ ηR)2
Among the formula, Ψ, I, A and η represented the QY, integral intensity, adsorption, and solvent refractive index, respectively.

The synthesized N-CQDs were examined for their selectivity and interferences in different food additives and metal ions presence at 1 mg/mL concentration, including rose essence powder, cyclamate, composite amino acids, lemon yellow, sunset yellow, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and so on. About 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution including 20 μL N-CQDs was mixed with 1 mL of different food additive or metal ion salt solution (1 mg/mL) and incubated at ambient temperature for 1 min. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity was observed at 422 nm. The high quenching lemon yellow were further determined at lower concentration levels.
GATORADE was used as a drink sample, which was provided by a local supermarket and used without any preparation.

As described in Scheme 1 , N-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method with dried pomelo peel and L-tyrosine. The morphology and size of N-CQDs particles were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the N-CQDs particles are spherical and uniformly distributed in the sample. As shown in Fig. 1b, the size distribution of N-CQDs ranges from 3.0 to 8.0 nm, with an average diameter of 4.8 nm.

The functional groups of the N-CQDs were characterized by FT-IR spectrum measurement. As shown in Fig. 1c, the broad peak at 3209 cm−1 is originated from the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, respectively [26]. The peak at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to symmetric stretching vibration of C–H [27]. The N-CQDs show a sharp band at 1588 cm−1 corresponding to bending vibrations of C = C of L-tyrosine on the surface of N-CQDs [28]. The peaks observe at 1367 cm−1 and 1325 cm−1 correspond to C-O stretching vibrations of –COOH [29]. The signal peak at 1244 cm−1 shows a sharp band which is related to C-O stretching from C-OH of L-tyrosine [30].

The element composition of N-CQDs was confirmed by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, three clear peaks around 285.36 eV, 400.87 eV and 532.14 eV could be ascribed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively [31]. The C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals that four components are existed with binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.32 eV and 288.57 eV, corresponding to C = C/C–C, C–O/C-N and C = O bonds, respectively [32,33]. The spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2c) contains only one peak, relating to N–H (400.87 eV) [34]. The peak 532.14 eV from the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) is assigned to C-O groups [35]. The above IR and XPS results suggest that hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH) and amino (–NH2) functional groups exist on the surface of N-CQDs.

To investigate optical properties of N-CQDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy of N-CQDs were recorded in phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.2). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the N-CQDs have two prominent absorption peaks at wavelength 220 nm and 270 nm, which correspond to the π −π* electronic transition of C = C and n-π* electronic transition of C = O, respectively [36,37]. Besides, the optimal excitation wavelength is 330 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength is 422 nm (Fig. 3b).

It is widely known that the pH of the solution is very important for detection. Phosphate buffer solution with different pH values (pH = 2.0–11.0) is selected for comparison. Fig. 4 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs remained unchanged between 7.0 and 11.0 of pH. Considering our physiological pH, pH 7.2 is selected as the substrate solution for detection.

In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of N-CQDs towards lemon yellow, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs were recorded with adding different food additives and some metal ions, such as lemon yellow, sunset yellow, D-isoascorbic acid, sucrose, vanillin, rose essence powder, cyclamate, compound amino acid, potassium chloride, ferrous sulfate, sucralose, anhydrous glucose, L-cysteine, calcium chloride, reduced glutathione, copper acetate, zinc chloride, etc. As shown in Fig. 5 a, only lemon yellow can significantly quench the fluorescence intensities of the aqueous N-CQDs solution, and has a stronger interaction with the N-CQDs than others. Therefore, it suggested that N-CQDs possess good selectivity.

To further evaluate the selectivity of N-CQDs toward lemon yellow, each interfering substance was added to the N-CQDs-lemon yellow mixture solution, the fluorescence intensity of which was recorded under the above-mentioned conditions. As shown in Fig. 5b, most interfering substances show no significant variation in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs-lemon yellow mixture solution. These data indicate that the N-CQDs display excellent selectivity toward lemon yellow in pH 7.2 phosphate buffer solution.

In order to investigate the quenching ability of lemon yellow on the N-CODs fluorescence, the fluorescence spectra of the N-CQDs were scanned after adding different concentrations of lemon yellow in deionized water. When excited at 330 nm, a strong emission peak at 422 nm can be observed (Fig. 6 a), the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs gradually decrease with gradual addition of lemon yellow along with an apparent red shift with 60 nm in the maximum wavelength. As seen in Fig. 6b and inset, a good linear relationship is observed in the range of 0–1.2 mg/L with a limit detection of 0.023 mg/L according to 3δ/s [38], which is lower than that of national standard. The linear fitting equation is F = -661c + 230 (R2 = 0.993, F represents the fluorescent intensity at 422 nm). The fluorescence quantum yield of the N-CQDs was calculated to be 28 %.

Generally speaking, fluorescence quantum yield could be reduced by various molecular interactions between N-CQDs and quencher molecules, such as electron or energy transfer, collision quenching, excited-state reactions, and the formation of ground state complexes [39,40].The quenching mechanism is usually divided into dynamic quenching and static quenching. Dynamic quenching can be caused by collision when the fluorescent groups of pigment molecules and carbon dots interacted in the excited state [41].Static quenching can be caused by ground state complexes because of the interaction between pigment molecules and fluorescent groups of carbon dots in the ground state [42].In addition, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer and internal filtration effect caused by pigment molecules can also lead to fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs (Fig. 7 ).

The fluorescence decay curve for the aqueous N-CQDs solution was fitted to obtain the lifetime (Fig. 7). When N-CQDs and lemon yellow were added, the lifetime did not change. These results show that the fluorescence quenching in these processes is the static quenching [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of lemon yellow in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of N-CQDs was tested for the detection of lemon yellow in GATORADE drink. The detected data are listed in Table 1 . The determination of the actual content of lemon yellow in soft drinks is based on the national standard method (GB 4481.1–2010). The standard curve equation of lemon yellow (y = -3346.9x + 836.83, R2 = 0.9938) was obtained by fluorescence detection. The accuracy and precision of the methods were confirmed by the values of recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD), which ranged from 90.68 % to 91.48 % and 1.9 to 2.7 %, respectively. The results indicate that our N-CQDs have good accuracy, reliability and practicability, and could be successfully applied to the determination of lemon yellow in the drink sample.

In summary, the new N-CQDs for the detection of lemon yellow was constructed successfully. The synthetic method of N-CQDs with pomelo peel as carbon sources is simple, efficient, green and low-cost. The results show that the obtained N-CQDs can be used to detect lemon yellow with high sensitivity and selectivity. The detection limit is 0.023 mg/L. It is clear that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs-lemon yellow system can be attributed to static quenching. Furthermore, the N-CQDs have been successfully used for the analysis of drink samples. Compared to other methods, the synthetic method of N-CQDs is simple, quick and low cost. Consequently, the N-CQDs can be applied as a promising sensor in food analysis.

Xiu-Juan Jiang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yuan Ma: Project administration. You Zhou: Data curation. Rong-Dan Xiao: Data curation. Yi-Jie Meng: Data curation. Ye-Hou: . Ben-Ting Xie: Formal analysis. Lin-Hong Wu: Data curation. De-Hong Zhao: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by College Students’ innovation and entrepreneurship training program of Sichuan, PR China (No. S202110622056).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lemon yellow #15 a new highly stable, water soluble food colorant from the peel of Citrus limon
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Chen, Y. Ding, B. Forrest, et al. Lemon yellow #15 a new highly stable, water soluble food colorant from the peel of Citrus limon, Food Chem. 270(2019) 251−256. Doi: 10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.07.055.

Anal. Methods-Uk

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xu, Q. Ding. Magnetic field induced high-density SERS active assembly of Fe3O4@Au nanostars in a glass capillary for food colorant detection, Anal. Methods-uk 13(2021) 5487–5492. Doi: 10.1039/D1AY00989C.

ACS Omega

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of sunset yellow and tartrazine in food samples by combining ionic liquid-based aqueous two-phase system with high performance liquid chromatography
J. Anal. Methods Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Sha, X.S. Zhu, Y.L. Feng, et al. Determination of sunset yellow and tartrazine in food samples by combining ionic liquid-based aqueous two-phase system with high performance liquid chromatography, J. Anal. Methods Chem. 2014(2014) 964273. Doi: 10.1155/2014 /964273.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: X.Y. Zhao, Y. Z. Liu, J.J. ZUO, et al. Rapid and sensitive determination of tartrazine using a molecularly imprinted copolymer modified carbon electrode (MIP-PmDB/PoPD-GCE), J. Electroanal. Chem. 785(2017) 90-95. Doi: 10.1016/j.jelechem.2016.12.015.

Doi: 10.1007/s00604-018-3047-4.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer
Environ. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nagaraj, S. Ramalingam, C. Murugan, et al. Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer, Environ. Res. 212(2022) 113273–113280. Doi: 10.1016/j.envres.2022. 113273.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mannitol capped magnetic dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of polar drugs sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin from milk and water samples followed by selective fluorescence sensing using boron-doped carbon quantum dots
J. Environ. Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.R.H. Ali, A.I. Hassan, Y. F. Hassan, et al. Mannitol capped magnetic dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of polar drugs sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin from milk and water samples followed by selective fluorescence sensing using boron-doped carbon quantum dots, J. Environ. Chem. Eng. 9(2021) 105078–105090. Doi: 10.1016/j.jece. 2021.105078.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots with enhanced sensitive for the determination of myricetin in real samples
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.Z. Liu, Z. Mi, Z.Y. Guo, et al. A label-free fluorescent sensor based on carbon quantum dots with enhanced sensitive for the determination of myricetin in real samples, Microchem. J. 157(2020) 104956–104962. Doi: 10.1016/j.microc.2020.104956.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Liao, X.Y. Zhao, F.W. Zhu, et al. Novel S, N-doped carbon quantum dot-based “off-on” fluorescent sensor for silver ion and cysteine, Talanta, 180(2018) 300–308. Doi: 10.101 6/j.talanta.2017.12.040.

Doi: 10.1016/ j.indcrop. 2022.114957.
